unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: In this unit, your child learns about the farm. They name farm animals and the sounds they make, learn farm words like tractor, barn and field, and talk about what people do on a farm — feeding, planting and picking. They meet the idea that animals and plants grow (a chick from an egg, a bean from a seed), and they hear a gentle story about helping and hard work. The goal is warm, confident spoken English — not perfect spelling.
unit.learningPath:
Begin with the guided teacher launch and learn a few words through pictures and play.
Listen to the Unit story and join in with repeated language.
Complete six language, speaking, early-writing and practical activities.
Meet your teacher three times each week for two weeks.
Finish the ten-question checkpoint and tell an adult what you can do.

visual.alt: Child-friendly illustration for On the Farm

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A big farm animal that gives us milk.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The cow eats grass in the field.
practiceSentences: The brown cow is very gentle.
Have you ever milked a cow?
Musa, that cow is huge!
I give fresh hay to the cow.
A cow says moo in the barn.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: c - o - w
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say cow slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A female farm bird that lays eggs for us.

I collect eggs from our hen.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: h - e - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say hen slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say chick slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say duck slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name common farm animals (cow, hen, chick, duck, sheep, goat, horse…).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say the sound each animal makes (moo, cluck, quack, baa…).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name farm places and things (farm, barn, field, tractor, egg, seed).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say what people and animals are doing: “The hen is eating.”
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Tell the simple life cycle of a hen (egg → chick → hen).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Join in the “Old MacDonald” song and the farm poem.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

readings.title: Amal and the Little Hen
readings.theme: farm animals, sounds, growing and work
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript:
Amal lived near a small farm. Her grandmother, Ayeeyo, kept animals there. Amal loved to visit every morning before the sun grew hot.

“Come, Amal,” said Ayeeyo. “There is much work to do on the farm today.”

First they went to see the cow. “Good morning, cow!” said Amal. The big brown cow said, “Moo!”

Next they saw the sheep and the goat. The sheep said, “Baa!” The goat wanted to eat Amal's hat! Amal laughed and laughed.

“Now we must feed the hens,” said Ayeeyo. She gave Amal a small bowl of seed.

Amal walked to the barn. The little hen looked hungry. “The hen is hungry,” said Amal. “I will feed her.” She threw the seed on the ground. “Cluck, cluck!” said the happy hen.

Then Amal saw something warm in the straw. It was a little white egg!

“Ayeeyo, look!” called Amal. “The hen gave us an egg!”

“Well done, Amal,” said Ayeeyo. “When you are kind to the animals, they are kind to you.”

In the afternoon, they worked in the field. Amal helped to plant a tiny seed in the ground. “Will it grow?” she asked.

“Yes,” said Ayeeyo. “With water and sun, the seed will grow into a big bean plant. Everything on a farm grows — the chick grows from an egg, and the bean grows from a seed.”

Her big brother Ishmael drove past on the tractor. “Hard work, little sister!” he called. Amal waved. She was tired, but she was happy.

That night, Amal ate warm bread and drank fresh milk from the cow. “I love the farm, Ayeeyo,” she said softly.

Ayeeyo smiled. “And the farm loves you, Amal. Good animals need good, kind children like you.”

readings.title: Talk about On the Farm
readings.theme: farm animals, sounds, growing and work
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript: Look, point and talk. This is a ___. The ___ says ___. The ___ is ___ing. It has ___. First ___, then ___. I can see a ___. An adult reads each line while the learner points, repeats and acts.

readings.title: On the Farm rhyme
readings.theme: farm animals, sounds, growing and work
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript:
“Old MacDonald Had a Farm” — sing it together
Old MacDonald had a farm, E-I-E-I-O. / And on that farm he had a cow, E-I-E-I-O. / With a moo moo here and a moo moo there, / Here a moo, there a moo, everywhere a moo moo! / Old MacDonald had a farm, E-I-E-I-O.
Sing a new verse for each animal your child names. Make the animal sound loudly and happily each time — a hen (cluck), a duck (quack), a sheep (baa).
The “Farm Poem” — say it together
Hello to the farmer, / Hello to the tractor, / Hello to the cows in their stall. / Hello to the fields, / Hello to the chicks, / Hello to you all!
Point to each thing (the tractor, the cows, the fields, the chicks) as you say its name.

comprehension.question: Which animal did Amal and Ayeeyo go to see first?
comprehension.explanation: The story says, 'First they went to see the cow.'

comprehension.question: Which animal wanted to eat Amal's hat?
comprehension.explanation: The story says the goat wanted to eat Amal's hat, and Amal laughed.

comprehension.question: What did Amal find in the straw after she fed the hen?
comprehension.explanation: Amal saw something warm in the straw: it was a little white egg.

comprehension.question: Ayeeyo says a seed needs two things to grow. Name them.
comprehension.explanation: Ayeeyo says, 'With water and sun, the seed will grow into a big bean plant.'

comprehension.question: How did Amal feel at the end of her farm day?
comprehension.explanation: After waving to Ishmael the story says she was tired, but she was happy.

comprehension.question: Why did the hen give Amal an egg?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because Amal was kind and fed her
comprehension.explanation: Ayeeyo says, 'When you are kind to the animals, they are kind to you.'

comprehension.question: Which talk line makes an animal sound?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The ___ says ___. (for example: The cow says moo.)
comprehension.explanation: 'The ___ says ___' is the line for animal sounds.

comprehension.question: Say 'First ___, then ___' about feeding the hen.
comprehension.correctAnswer: First I get the seed, then I feed the hen.
comprehension.explanation: 'First ___, then ___' is the talk line for putting two steps in order.

comprehension.question: Which talk line tells what an animal is doing now?
comprehension.explanation: 'The ___ is ___ing' says what is happening right now.

comprehension.question: Say 'It has ___' about a goat.
comprehension.explanation: 'It has ___' is the talk line for describing the parts of an animal.

comprehension.question: Look at the farm picture and finish: 'I can see a ___.'
comprehension.correctAnswer: I can see a hen. (any animal in the picture)
comprehension.explanation: 'I can see a ___' is the last talk line, for naming what you see.

comprehension.question: Point to one farm animal and say, 'This is a ___.'
comprehension.correctAnswer: This is a sheep. (any animal named correctly)
comprehension.explanation: The first talk line is 'This is a ___,' for pointing and naming.

grammar.title: Name a farm animal
grammar.explanation: Name one farm animal. Say 'a' before most words, but 'an' before a vowel sound: a cow, an ox.
grammar.commonMistake: Children pop 'a' before ox by mistake. Before an a, e, i, o, u sound we say 'an': an ox, an egg. Say it slowly together.
grammar.memoryTip: Open-mouth sound at the start — a, e, i, o, u — means you say 'an'.
grammar.practice: Name three: This is a cow. This is an ox. This is a hen.

grammar.title: Match the animal to its sound
grammar.explanation: Name the animal first, then copy the sound it makes. Make the sound nice and big!
grammar.commonMistake: Children sometimes give only the sound, like 'moo'. Ask who makes it, then say the whole sentence.
grammar.memoryTip: Say the animal, take a breath, then let the sound out loud.
grammar.practice: Say three animal sounds: The cow says moo. The sheep says baa. The duck says quack.

grammar.title: Say what a farm animal is doing
grammar.explanation: Watch one animal and describe what it is doing now. Add -ing to the action word.
grammar.commonMistake: 'The hen pecking' is missing 'is'. Every 'happening now' sentence needs 'is' before the -ing word.
grammar.memoryTip: Point at the animal, say 'is', then say the busy -ing word.
grammar.practice: Describe three animals: The hen is pecking. The cow is eating. The goat is running.

grammar.title: Describe what an animal has
grammar.explanation: Tell us the parts an animal has, like horns, wings or wool. Say 'It has' and add the part.
grammar.commonMistake: Children often say 'It have wings.' When we talk about one animal we say 'It has wings.'
grammar.memoryTip: Touch that part on your picture or model as you say 'It has'.
grammar.practice: Describe three animals: It has horns. It has wings. It has soft wool.

grammar.title: Put two farm jobs in order
grammar.explanation: Say which farm job happens first, then say what happens next. Order words keep the story clear.
grammar.commonMistake: Sometimes the two steps come out muddled. Ask 'Which one happened first?' before saying the sentence.
grammar.memoryTip: Hold up one finger for 'first' and two fingers for 'then'.
grammar.practice: Say three ordered pairs: First we feed the hens, then we collect the eggs. First we milk the cow, then we clean the shed. First we plant seeds, then we water them.

grammar.title: Spot and name what you see on the farm
grammar.explanation: Name one thing you spot on the farm. Say 'a' before most words, but 'an' before a vowel sound: a barn, an ox.
grammar.commonMistake: Children pop 'a' before ox by mistake. Before an a, e, i, o, u sound we say 'an': an ox, an apple tree.
grammar.memoryTip: Shade your eyes, then listen: open-mouth start — a, e, i, o, u — use 'an'.
grammar.practice: Spot and say three: I can see a barn. I can see an ox. I can see a sheep.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 - Listen and point
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Point to farm animals as an adult names them, then name them on your own. Say: 'This is a cow.' 'This is a hen.' 'This is a sheep.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern This is a ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 - Repeat the model
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Copy the adult's animal sound, then try one by yourself. Say: 'The cow says moo.' 'The hen says cluck.' 'The sheep says baa.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern The ___ says ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 - Ask and answer
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Ask and answer about what the farm animals are doing. Say: 'What is the duck doing?' 'The duck is swimming.' 'The goat is eating.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern The ___ is ___ing. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 - Picture talk
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Look at a farm picture and describe one animal's body. Say: 'It has four legs.' 'It has soft wool.' 'It has two wings.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern It has ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 - Role-play
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Act out the farmer's morning with a partner. Say: 'First we feed the hens, then we collect the eggs.' 'First we milk the cow, then we rest.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern First ___, then ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 - My confident turn
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Take your own turn describing a busy farm picture. Say: 'I can see a goat.' 'I can see a big field.' 'I can see three ducks.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern I can see a ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

writing.title: Writing 1 - Draw and tell
writing.promptAndInstructions: Draw a farm with two animals and one plant growing. Tell an adult what the farmer does every morning.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 2 - Trace
writing.promptAndInstructions: Trace the words 'cow' and 'hen'. Make that animal's sound as you finish each word, then trace both once more.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 3 - Match and copy
writing.promptAndInstructions: Join duck, goat and sheep to the right animal pictures. Now copy the name of the one that can swim.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 4 - Label
writing.promptAndInstructions: Add two labels to your farm drawing: 'egg' and 'field'. Draw a short arrow from each word to the right place.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 5 - Complete a pattern
writing.promptAndInstructions: Copy and finish: 'First ___, then ___.' Write about feeding the hens and collecting the eggs. Read it back in the right order.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 6 - Create one sentence
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose a farm picture. Write one sentence: 'I can see a ___.' Then count that animal and tell an adult how many.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

activities.title: Activity 1
activities.instructionsAndItems: Colour the farm animals: the pig pink, the cow brown, the chick yellow and the duck white. Point to each one and say its name.
activities.answerSummary: Pig pink, cow brown, chick yellow and duck white, with each animal pointed to and named. The white duck may be left blank or lightly outlined and still counts. Approximate shades are fine.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 2
activities.instructionsAndItems: Draw your favourite farm animal big and bright. Then say: 'This is a ___. It says ___. It has ___ legs.'
activities.answerSummary: One farm animal drawn big and bright, then the three spoken parts: its name, its sound and its number of legs. Four legs for a cow or sheep, two for a hen or duck.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 3
activities.instructionsAndItems: Draw a line from each animal to its sound: cow to moo, sheep to baa, duck to quack, hen to cluck. Make every sound aloud.
activities.answerSummary: The correct joins are cow to moo, sheep to baa, duck to quack and hen to cluck. Accept every sound made with gusto; making the noise shows they know the answer.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 4
activities.instructionsAndItems: Move like the farm animals. 1. Waddle like a duck. 2. Hop like a lamb. 3. Peck like a hen. Name each animal as you move.
activities.answerSummary: Watch for a duck waddle, a lamb hop and a hen peck, each animal named. Accept a loose imitation, and the name said before or after the movement, whichever comes more easily.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 5
activities.instructionsAndItems: Point to things on the farm picture: barn, tractor, field, pond. Say 'I can see a ___' for each one you find.
activities.answerSummary: Barn, tractor, field and pond all found in the farm picture and named with 'I can see a ___'. Accept 'grass' for field or 'water' for pond, then supply the exact word warmly.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 6
activities.instructionsAndItems: Grow a bean. 1. Put cotton wool in a cup. 2. Add a bean and a little water. 3. Look every day. Tell an adult what changed.
activities.answerSummary: Cotton wool placed in a cup, a bean added with a little water, and a daily look. Success is telling you one change, such as 'it is bigger', or pointing at a sprouted root.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

assignments.title: My On the Farm show-and-tell page
assignments.instructions: Build a little farm. 1) Draw a farm, or make animals from paper or playdough. 2) Label two things with traced words: cow, hen, barn, field or tractor. 3) Record yourself saying: This is a ___. The cow says moo. The hen is eating. It has two legs. Make two animal sounds out loud. 4) Add one growing sentence: First we plant the seed, then it grows. 5) Grown-ups, write which farm job your child liked best.
assignments.submissionType: Drawing or object + labels + short audio/video + reflection

quizzes.question: Which word names a farm animal?
quizzes.explanation: A goat is an animal; a tractor, a barn and grass are all found on a farm but they are not alive animals.

quizzes.question: Which farm animal gives us milk?
quizzes.explanation: A cow gives milk; a hen and a duck give eggs and we do not milk a horse.

quizzes.question: Which farm animal lays the eggs we eat for breakfast?
quizzes.explanation: A hen lays eggs; a sheep, a cow and a horse do not lay eggs at all.

quizzes.question: What sound does a cow make?
quizzes.explanation: A cow says moo; quack is a duck, baa is a sheep and neigh is a horse.

quizzes.question: Which animal says baa?
quizzes.explanation: A sheep says baa; a duck quacks, a cow moos and a hen clucks.

quizzes.question: What do we call a baby hen?
quizzes.explanation: A baby hen is a chick; a calf is a baby cow, a lamb is a baby sheep and a foal is a baby horse.

quizzes.question: Which farm animal can swim on the pond?
quizzes.explanation: A duck swims on water; hens, sheep and goats stay on the land.

quizzes.question: Which sentence tells what the horse is doing right now?
quizzes.options: This is a horse. | The horse is running. | The horse is brown. | The horse has a long tail.
quizzes.explanation: The -ing word running shows an action happening now; the others tell us a colour, a body part or a name.

quizzes.question: Which sentence tells us what a sheep has?
quizzes.options: It has feathers. | It has wings. | It has soft wool. | It has fins.
quizzes.explanation: Sheep are covered in wool; feathers and wings belong to birds and fins belong to fish.

quizzes.question: Which word do we use to tell what happens at the beginning of a story?
quizzes.explanation: First shows the beginning; then and next come in the middle and last comes at the end.

liveSessions.title: Meet the words
liveSessions.beforeSession: Bring crayons and one real object connected to the Unit.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min hello song; 7 min model and repeat; 8 min game or story; 7 min guided practice; 3 min celebrate and preview
liveSessions.afterSession: Show one word, action, drawing or sentence from On the Farm to an adult.

teacherNotes.note: Use 15-20 minute independent blocks inside the lesson. Read every instruction aloud, model before expecting a response, and accept pointing, acting, drawing, tracing or speaking as valid evidence.
A teacher or caregiver remains present whenever the young learner uses recording or AI tutor features. The adult controls the device and protects the child's personal information.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Answer: The cow. The story says, 'First they went to see the cow.' Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: The goat. The story says the goat wanted to eat Amal's hat, and Amal laughed. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: A little white egg. Amal saw something warm in the straw: it was a little white egg. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: Water and sun. Ayeeyo says, 'With water and sun, the seed will grow into a big bean plant.' Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: Tired, but happy. After waving to Ishmael the story says she was tired, but she was happy. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: Because Amal was kind and fed her. Ayeeyo says, 'When you are kind to the animals, they are kind to you.' Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: The ___ says ___. (for example: The cow says moo.). 'The ___ says ___' is the line for animal sounds. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: First I get the seed, then I feed the hen. 'First ___, then ___' is the talk line for putting two steps in order. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: The ___ is ___ing. 'The ___ is ___ing' says what is happening right now. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: It has horns. (any true part). 'It has ___' is the talk line for describing the parts of an animal. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: I can see a hen. (any animal in the picture). 'I can see a ___' is the last talk line, for naming what you see. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: This is a sheep. (any animal named correctly). The first talk line is 'This is a ___,' for pointing and naming. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “This is a / an ___.”. Model answers: This is a cow. This is an ox. This is a hen. Watch for: Children pop 'a' before ox by mistake. Before an a, e, i, o, u sound we say 'an': an ox, an egg. Say it slowly together.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “The ___ says ___.”. Model answers: The cow says moo. The sheep says baa. The duck says quack. Watch for: Children sometimes give only the sound, like 'moo'. Ask who makes it, then say the whole sentence.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “The ___ is ___ing.”. Model answers: The hen is pecking. The cow is eating. The goat is running. Watch for: 'The hen pecking' is missing 'is'. Every 'happening now' sentence needs 'is' before the -ing word.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “It has ___.”. Model answers: It has horns. It has wings. It has soft wool. Watch for: Children often say 'It have wings.' When we talk about one animal we say 'It has wings.'

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “First ___, then ___.”. Model answers: First we feed the hens, then we collect the eggs. First we milk the cow, then we clean the shed. First we plant seeds, then we water them. Watch for: Sometimes the two steps come out muddled. Ask 'Which one happened first?' before saying the sentence.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “I can see a / an ___.”. Model answers: I can see a barn. I can see an ox. I can see a sheep. Watch for: Children pop 'a' before ox by mistake. Before an a, e, i, o, u sound we say 'an': an ox, an apple tree.
Pig pink, cow brown, chick yellow and duck white, with each animal pointed to and named. The white duck may be left blank or lightly outlined and still counts. Approximate shades are fine.
One farm animal drawn big and bright, then the three spoken parts: its name, its sound and its number of legs. Four legs for a cow or sheep, two for a hen or duck.
The correct joins are cow to moo, sheep to baa, duck to quack and hen to cluck. Accept every sound made with gusto; making the noise shows they know the answer.
Watch for a duck waddle, a lamb hop and a hen peck, each animal named. Accept a loose imitation, and the name said before or after the movement, whichever comes more easily.
Barn, tractor, field and pond all found in the farm picture and named with 'I can see a ___'. Accept 'grass' for field or 'water' for pond, then supply the exact word warmly.
Cotton wool placed in a cup, a bean added with a little water, and a daily look. Success is telling you one change, such as 'it is bigger', or pointing at a sprouted root.
Correct option: goat. A goat is an animal; a tractor, a barn and grass are all found on a farm but they are not alive animals.
Correct option: cow. A cow gives milk; a hen and a duck give eggs and we do not milk a horse.
Correct option: hen. A hen lays eggs; a sheep, a cow and a horse do not lay eggs at all.
Correct option: moo. A cow says moo; quack is a duck, baa is a sheep and neigh is a horse.
Correct option: sheep. A sheep says baa; a duck quacks, a cow moos and a hen clucks.
Correct option: chick. A baby hen is a chick; a calf is a baby cow, a lamb is a baby sheep and a foal is a baby horse.
Correct option: duck. A duck swims on water; hens, sheep and goats stay on the land.
Correct option: The horse is running.. The -ing word running shows an action happening now; the others tell us a colour, a body part or a name.
Correct option: It has soft wool.. Sheep are covered in wool; feathers and wings belong to birds and fins belong to fish.
Correct option: First. First shows the beginning; then and next come in the middle and last comes at the end.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name common farm animals (cow, hen, chick, duck, sheep, goat, horse…).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say the sound each animal makes (moo, cluck, quack, baa…).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can name farm places and things (farm, barn, field, tractor, egg, seed).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say what people and animals are doing: “The hen is eating.”.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can tell the simple life cycle of a hen (egg → chick → hen).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can join in the “Old MacDonald” song and the farm poem.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

rubrics.level1: Marks do not yet show the idea
rubrics.level3: Labels or copies a meaningful phrase
Uses a capital or end mark with support
rubrics.level4: Uses a capital and end mark independently
